arXiv.title: Steering a warp drive without exotic matter
arXiv.abstract: A useful warp drive must change its velocity, yet known positive-energy constructions are static or constant-velocity, while accelerating ones require exotic matter. As Bobrick and Martire noted, warp drives "do not have any natural way of changing their velocities." We first settle the question of principle by reading the Bondi-Sachs balance law as a propulsion law: any asymptotically flat drive with a confined, dominant-energy material source (with standard peeling) can change its Bondi four-momentum only by radiating to null infinity, so steering requires radiation, the general-relativistic counterpart of the relativistic rocket. We then exhibit a positive-energy spacetime that saturates it. Prescribing the passenger worldtube and taking the exact Kinnersley photon rocket as the exterior, we solve for the matching timelike shell: the mechanism is photon-rocket recoil around a tidally protected flat cavity, whose flat interior, not a warp field, is the defining warp-drive property. The exterior satisfies the energy conditions whenever $n^2 \ge 0$, and steering obeys the closed-form control law $-\dot{m} \ge 3m|a|$, paid entirely by Bondi mass loss. The static matched shell satisfies the surface dominant energy condition for $2m/R < 24/25$, and accelerating configurations exist unconditionally for small acceleration, below a rigorous closed-form acceleration-compactness bound. The least-radiating maneuver is the gravitational-wave-silent Damour dipole, and minimum-radiation steering is a hyperbolic geodesic with the universal Tsiolkovsky constant $3$. A slow fixed-axis burn assembles perturbatively into a time-evolved spacetime. The drive is causal, subluminal, and a reaction drive: steering costs an order-unity fraction of the rest mass for mildly relativistic boosts, yet needs no exotic matter. Steering a warp drive is a problem of energy budget, not negative energy.
